messages.content: The article is devoted to a hitherto overlooked architectural motif of spherical supports in tower helmets, observed in northern Europe since the early 17th century. Probably derived from the obelisk ball-based motif popular in iconography, it first appeared within the helmet in the Westerkerk tower designed by Hendrik de Keyser, and later in the realizations of his students in Denmark. Graphic representations of the towers also played a role in popularizing the motif. The text traces the possible paths of the spread of the new form and the possible connections between the various objects. The varied forms and contexts of use of the motif in question did not make it possible to identify additional ideological content of the objects, beyond the prestigious functions naturally associated with tower dominants. - rephrase this

This article explores a previously overlooked architectural motif found in tower helmets in northern Europe since the early 17th century. The motif, which consists of spherical supports, may have been derived from the popular obelisk ball-based motif in iconography. It first appeared in the Westerkerk tower designed by Hendrik de Keyser, and later in the works of his students in Denmark. The motif was also popularized through graphic representations of the towers. The article investigates the potential routes of the motif's spread and explores possible connections between the different objects. Due to the various forms and contexts in which the motif was used, it is difficult to determine any additional ideological content beyond the prestigious functions associated with dominant tower structures.

W początkach XVII w. na terenie Danii, a następnie dalej, w regionie morza Bałtyckiego, na pograniczu Śląska i Łużyc, w Prusach Książęcych i Wielkim Księstwie Litewskim pojawił się motyw architektoniczny hełmów wieżowych, w których zamiast słupów bądź kolumienek stosowano podpory w kształcie kul. - rephrase this

In the early 17th century, a new architectural motif emerged in Denmark and later spread to the Baltic Sea region, on the border of Silesia and Lusatia, as well as in the duchy of Prussia and the Grand Duchy of Lithuania. This motif involved the use of spherical supports instead of pillars or columns in tower helmets.

Despite the extremely expressive form and rich iconography, it has not been possible to identify the ideological rationale behind the use of the motif They can therefore be seen as a manifestation of prestige, associated with the ornate form, the seemingly athectonic structure and references to buildings from the most important cities in the region. To this day, the forms of the helmets give the impression of breaking the laws of statics, so one can only surmise how strongly influential these powerful towers, balancing wobbly on golden spheres, were in the past. - rephrase this

Despite their visually striking form and intricate iconography, the underlying ideological significance of the motif remains elusive. However, it is clear that the use of the motif reflected a desire for prestige, as evidenced by the ornate design and allusions to prominent buildings in the region. Even today, the helmets' forms give the impression of defying the laws of gravity, leaving us to speculate on the profound impression these towering structures, precariously balanced on golden spheres, must have had in the past.

Badanie motywu sferycznych podpór hełmów podlega tym samym ograniczeniom, co badanie hełmów jako takich, choć w jeszcze większym stopniu. Są nimi: szczątkowy stan zachowania niegdyś znacznie liczniejszych obiektów, niedokładność ikonografii, nikły zasób źródeł pisanych i fragmentaryczność istniejących opracowań. Choć nawet wstępne przybliżenie stopnia zachowania nowożytnych hełmów w architekturze europejskiej wymagałoby wieloletnich, wnikliwych badań o charakterze regionalnym, to wskazać tu można, że analiza tego typu wykonana dla obszaru Śląska sugeruje zachowanie się około 10% spośród pierwotnej liczby konstrukcji nowożytnych . Ikonografia jest często nie dość szczegółowa i wierna aby oddać detale struktur zwieńczeń budowli, podobnie jak skąpe przekazy pisane. Pomimo tego, w toku prowadzonych badań zidentyfikowano ponad trzydzieści przykładów tego typu hełmów, zarówno zrealizowanych jak i projektowanych, rozmieszczonych głównie w rejonie południowego pobrzeża Bałtyku, cieśnin duńskich i wokół styku Śląska i Łużyc. - rephrase this

The study of the motif of spherical supports in helmets faces the same limitations as the study of helmets themselves, but to an even greater extent. These limitations include the fragmentary state of preservation of once numerous objects, imprecise iconography, sparse written sources, and the incomplete existing research on the topic. While even a preliminary assessment of the preservation of these helmets in European architecture would require extensive and in-depth regional studies, an analysis focused on the Silesian region suggests that approximately 10% of the original number of these structures may still exist. The iconography often lacks the necessary level of detail and fidelity to accurately depict the architectural crown details, and the written records are also scarce. Despite these challenges, ongoing research has identified over thirty examples of such helmets, both realized and proposed, primarily located in the southern Baltic coast, Danish straits, and the border region of Silesia and Lusatia.
Iconography is often insufficiently detailed and faithful to convey the details of building finial structures, as are the sparse written records. - rephrase this
The iconography often lacks the level of detail necessary to accurately depict the intricate finial structures of buildings, and the limited written records provide only scant information.
Iconography is often insufficiently detailed and faithful to convey the details of building finial structures, as are the sparse written records. - rephrase this

The iconography frequently fails to provide sufficient detail and accuracy when depicting the intricate finial structures of buildings, and the available written records are also sparse in their descriptions.

With some hesitation, we can also mention two other helmets that were also created by Christian IV. In 1629, in a fire at Kronborg Castle in Helsingør on the Sund Strait, four of the five tower helmets were destroyed. The stone finial of Kalkelborg - the tower of the southeast corner - survived . The surviving helmet allows negative verification of the assumption that the rebuilt helmets resemble their earlier counterparts . In the course of the decade-long reconstruction, Hans van Steenwinckel the younger, Laurens' brother and at the same time also a pupil of de Keyser, erected a two-tiered, two-story helmet, the first tier of which gives the impression of being carried by gilded spheres (il. 3). In reality, however, the spheres were placed slightly in front of the tambour facets and rested on obelisks. This solution also appeared earlier in the helmet of the Barbican in Frederiksborg (il. 4), erected by Hans van Steenwinckel the younger in 1621-23 , however, the illusion of the spheres being the support of the upper stories was not used there. The helmets of the barbican and the Kronborg tower - despite some similarity to the tower are also distinguished from the kirketårnet by the use of star-shaped tambours. -rephrase this

It is worth mentioning two additional helmets that were created by Christian IV with some hesitation. In 1629, a fire at Kronborg Castle in Helsingør on the Sund Strait resulted in the destruction of four out of the five tower helmets. However, the stone finial of Kalkelborg, located on the tower's southeast corner, miraculously survived. This surviving helmet allows us to verify that the rebuilt helmets did not closely resemble their earlier counterparts. During the extensive decade-long reconstruction, Hans van Steenwinckel the younger, the brother of Laurens and also a student of de Keyser, erected a two-tiered, two-story helmet. The first tier of this helmet gives the illusion of being supported by gilded spheres (illustration 3). However, in reality, the spheres were positioned slightly in front of the tambour facets and rested on obelisks. A similar solution was also employed in the helmet of the Barbican in Frederiksborg (illustration 4), which was built by Hans van Steenwinckel the younger between 1621 and 1623. However, in the case of the Barbican, the illusion of the spheres being the support for the upper stories was not utilized. Furthermore, both the helmets of the Barbican and the Kronborg tower, despite bearing some similarity to the tower helmets, stand out for their use of star-shaped tambours.

It is possible that Hans van Steenwinckel the younger also created the design for the new helmet of the collapsed tower of St. Nicholas Church in Copenhagen in 1628 (il. 5). The extent of the damage done by the falling spire translated into a lengthy reconstruction . After the event, the architect carried out repair work, leading to the construction of a new chancel, roofs and vaults. In 1639 the death of van Steenwinckel , nota bene buried in this church , caused the work to slow down considerably, and it was not until 1664 that the replacement of the previous makeshift pyramidal roof with a new finial, completed a year later, began. The creator of this multi-story spire was master carpenter Lauge Thommesen, executing a design by architect Albertus Matthiesen . It is difficult to determine to what extent Matthiesen influenced the shape of the tower, however, a comparison with the Amalienborg Palace, which he built in 1667-73 for Queen-in-law Sofia Amalia, leads one to believe that the interference was slight. Indeed, Amalienborg lacks the picturesque complexity that characterizes the works of the van Steenwinckel family - the tambours visible above the compact body of the palace were covered with simple, semicircular domes . The tower of St. Nicholas Church, on the other hand, represents a different type. Above the quadrilateral shaft of the tower there rose a helmet composed of five tapering tiers, canopies, bypasses, obelisks and openwork buttresses. It was distinguished from earlier helmets by its strongly bulging canopies - albeit also pierced by dormers. In graphic depictions, spherical buttresses can be seen in the middle tier. Placed above the domed canopy and covered by a roof with concave slopes, the tier with spheres was a far more independent element than the earlier examples. However, this solution - in the light of the collected examples - is rarer - the tier with spherical supports was far more often placed directly above or below the openwork transom with columns. - rephrase this

It is possible that Hans van Steenwinckel the younger was also responsible for the design of the new helmet for the collapsed tower of St. Nicholas Church in Copenhagen in 1628 (illustration 5). The extensive damage caused by the falling spire resulted in a lengthy reconstruction process. After the incident, the architect carried out repair work, which involved constructing a new chancel, roofs, and vaults. However, with van Steenwinckel's death in 1639 (buried in this very church), the progress of the project slowed down considerably. It was not until 1664 that the replacement of the previous makeshift pyramidal roof with a new finial began, a task completed a year later. Master carpenter Lauge Thommesen executed the design by architect Albertus Matthiesen. It is challenging to determine the extent of Matthiesen's influence on the tower's shape. However, a comparison with the Amalienborg Palace, which Matthiesen built between 1667 and 1673 for Queen-in-law Sofia Amalia, suggests that his interference was minimal. Unlike the picturesque complexity of the van Steenwinckel family's works, Amalienborg lacks tambours above its compact body and instead features simple, semicircular domes. On the other hand, the tower of St. Nicholas Church represents a different type of architecture. Above the quadrilateral shaft of the tower, there was a helmet consisting of five tapering tiers, canopies, bypasses, obelisks, and openwork buttresses. It was distinguished from earlier helmets by its prominently bulging canopies, though it still featured dormers. Graphic depictions show spherical buttresses in the middle tier. Placed above the domed canopy and covered by a roof with concave slopes, this tier with spheres was a more independent element compared to earlier examples. However, this solution, based on the collected examples, is relatively rare. More commonly, the tier with spherical supports was placed directly above or below the openwork transom with columns.

The period of existence of Wrangel's founded castle in Bremervörde was relatively short, as it was already damaged and looted by Danish troops in 1657, and in 1682 the demolition of the buildings began, lasting until the end of the century . However, it can be assumed that almost immediately after its completion in 1652, it became the inspiration for another residential building on the border between Denmark and the German countries. After the destruction of the old Lauenburg Castle in southern Holstein by Swedish troops in 1656, Prince Julius Heinrich von Sachsen-Lauenburg planned to erect a new residence. It is difficult to determine to what extent the grandiose project was realized, while the complex itself was largely destroyed, design graphics of the palace including the garden have survived . Among the planned structures was a recreational garden pavilion - Lusthaus - designed by Dionys Bredekov (il. 7). The pavilion was designed as an octagon with a stepped-back floor covered by a dome. Surrounding the octagon were four low, quadrilateral annexes on the main axes and four hexagonal turrets with domes at the other sides. The central dome was pierced at the base with eight oval dormers and topped with a lantern, with eight spheres acting as supports. Although the complex profile of the lantern finial and the elaborate figurative pennant are reminiscent of the helmets discussed earlier, the Lusthaus finial has a small number of elements of unmistakably Dutch origin, such as obelisks, buttresses detached from the walls or overhanging bypasses with balustrades. It is difficult to determine whether the pavilion designed by Bredekow is a creative transformation of the Bremervörde helmet, or is perhaps just a variant of some unknown Danish buildings . For, apart from the palace design, Bredekov himself is known as a military engineer working on the construction of the Carlstadt fortifications, and on duty in Bremen, Verden, Wismar and Hvalliskens , and other works of civilian architecture of his authorship have not yet been revealed. - rephrase it

The castle founded by Wrangel in Bremervörde had a relatively short lifespan, as it was damaged and looted by Danish troops in 1657. The demolition of the buildings began in 1682 and lasted until the end of the century. However, it can be assumed that shortly after its completion in 1652, it served as an inspiration for another residential building on the border between Denmark and the German territories. Following the destruction of the old Lauenburg Castle in southern Holstein by Swedish troops in 1656, Prince Julius Heinrich von Sachsen-Lauenburg planned to construct a new residence. The extent to which this grand project was realized is difficult to determine, as much of the complex was destroyed. However, design graphics of the palace and its gardens have survived. Among the structures planned was a recreational garden pavilion known as Lusthaus, designed by Dionys Bredekov (illustration 7). The pavilion was designed as an octagon with a recessed floor and a dome covering. Surrounding the octagon were four low quadrilateral annexes along the main axes, as well as four hexagonal turrets with domes on the other sides. The central dome was adorned with eight oval dormers at its base and topped with a lantern supported by eight spheres. While the elaborate profile of the lantern finial and the figurative pennant bear resemblance to the discussed helmets, the Lusthaus finial also incorporates distinct Dutch elements such as obelisks, detached buttresses, and overhanging bypasses with balustrades. It is challenging to determine whether Bredekow's pavilion is a creative transformation of the Bremervörde helmet or merely a variant of some unknown Danish buildings. Apart from the palace design, Bredekov is primarily known as a military engineer involved in constructing the Carlstadt fortifications and serving in several locations such as Bremen, Verden, Wismar, and Hvalliskens. His other works in civilian architecture have yet to be discovered.
